title.en: Cloud Storage: Classes, Lifecycle & Data Protection

description.en: Master GCS storage classes, lifecycle rules, versioning, and access patterns — the object store that underpins BigQuery exports, ML datasets, and every Vertex AI artifact.

story_variants.en:
## The Substrate of Everything

Before you train a model, you stage the dataset in a bucket. When BigQuery exports, it writes to a bucket. Vertex AI Pipelines drop every artifact — metrics, model files, lineage metadata — into a bucket. **Cloud Storage (GCS)** is the substrate of the entire GCP data and ML platform, so understanding its cost and durability model pays compound interest for the rest of this track.

GCS stores immutable **objects** in **buckets**. Bucket names are globally unique; objects are addressed as `gs://bucket-name/path/to/object`. The 'folders' you see in the console are a rendering of `/` in object names — the namespace is actually flat (hierarchical namespace buckets exist as an opt-in for filesystem-like semantics, mainly for analytics and AI workloads).

## Storage Classes: Same Durability, Different Economics

All four classes offer the same eleven-nines design durability and the same millisecond access latency. What changes is the **pricing model**: colder classes charge less for storage at rest but add per-GB retrieval fees and minimum storage durations.

| Class | Min storage duration | Intended access pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | none | Hot data, serving, active ML datasets |
| Nearline | 30 days | Accessed about once a month (recent backups) |
| Coldline | 90 days | Accessed about once a quarter |
| Archive | 365 days | Compliance retention, disaster recovery |

> **Pitfall:** 'Archive is cheapest' is only true if you actually leave data alone. Deleting or reading an Archive object early still incurs the minimum-duration charge and retrieval fees. Cold classes punish indecision.

There is also **Autoclass**: a bucket setting where GCS observes each object's access pattern and transitions it between classes automatically, removing retrieval fees for objects it moved. For teams unwilling to model access patterns, Autoclass is the honest default; for well-understood pipelines, explicit lifecycle rules are cheaper and predictable.

## Lifecycle Management: Policy as Robot

Lifecycle rules are conditions plus one action, evaluated daily per object:

- **Conditions**: `age` (days since creation), `createdBefore`, `matchesStorageClass`, `numNewerVersions`, `matchesPrefix`.
- **Actions**: `SetStorageClass` (demote to a colder class) or `Delete`.

A classic policy for log archives: at 30 days move to Nearline, at 90 to Coldline, at 365 to Archive, delete at 7 years. Rules can only move objects to **colder** classes — promotion back to Standard is a manual rewrite (or Autoclass's job).

> **Callout:** Lifecycle rules are the FinOps tool nobody assigns an owner to. Audit them quarterly: a bucket of ML training shards that quietly aged into Coldline will hit you with retrieval fees on the next full retrain.

## Data Protection: Versioning, Retention, Soft Delete

Three distinct mechanisms, often confused:

- **Object versioning** — overwrites and deletes archive the old **generation** instead of destroying it. Pair with a lifecycle rule on `numNewerVersions` so old generations don't accumulate forever.
- **Retention policies / bucket lock** — WORM semantics: objects cannot be deleted or overwritten until the retention period passes. Locking the policy makes it irreversible — regulators love it, so do ransomware defenders.
- **Soft delete** — a bucket-level window (enabled by default on new buckets) during which deleted objects are recoverable, independent of versioning.

Add **dual-region** or **multi-region** buckets for geo-redundancy with a single endpoint; turbo replication gives a 15-minute RPO target for dual-region.

## Access Control: Pick Uniform, Almost Always

Buckets have two ACL modes. **Uniform bucket-level access** disables per-object ACLs and lets IAM alone decide — this is the recommended, auditable posture and the one org policies commonly enforce. Fine-grained (legacy ACL) mode survives for narrow interop cases.

For sharing without principals, **signed URLs** grant time-boxed access to a single object using a service account's signing key — this is how you let a browser upload a 2 GB video without proxying bytes through your backend.

Performance notes that bite at scale:

- Sequential object names (`log-000001`, `log-000002`...) used to hotspot; modern GCS autoscales indexing, but prefix diversity still helps at extreme write QPS.
- Use **composite objects / parallel composite uploads** for large files; the `gcloud storage` CLI (successor to `gsutil`) does this automatically and is dramatically faster.
- Class A operations (writes, lists) cost more than class B (reads) — list-heavy sync jobs are a hidden cost center.

## Where This Meets Vertex AI

Every Vertex AI custom training job mounts or reads `gs://` URIs; pipeline roots are GCS paths; model artifacts land as `saved_model/` directories in buckets. Standard class in the same region as your training compute is the rule — cross-region reads add latency and egress-style charges to every epoch.

## What You'll Build

The challenge implements a lifecycle rule engine in miniature: given object ages, apply the 30/90/365-day transition policy and report each object's fate. It's the same first-match-wins evaluation you'll later write as JSON in a real bucket's lifecycle configuration — practiced here where you can unit-test it.

description.en: A bucket bundles policy: class transitions, versioning, and access control all attach here.
Lifecycle rules only move objects rightward — colder and cheaper at rest, pricier to touch.

left.label: Guess a class once and never revisit it

right.label: Autoclass for unknown patterns, explicit lifecycle rules for known ones

description.en: Match the mechanism to how well you understand the access pattern.

explanation.en: A lifecycle rule is a small JSON document attached to the bucket.
SetStorageClass demotes the object; Delete is the other available action.
age counts days since object creation; conditions AND together within one rule.

lines: $ gcloud storage ls -L gs://pym-ml-artifacts/model.tar.gz | grep -i class

description.en: Demotion is automatic via rules; promotion back to Standard is an explicit rewrite.

instruction.en: Implement a GCS lifecycle rule engine. All objects start in STANDARD. Rules (first match wins, coldest first): age >= 365 -> ARCHIVE; age >= 90 -> COLDLINE; age >= 30 -> NEARLINE; otherwise no change. Implement target_class(age_days) returning the class name string ('STANDARD' if no transition). For each object in OBJECTS, in order, print exactly: '<name> (<age>d): STANDARD -> <target>' when the class changes, or '<name> (<age>d): STANDARD (no change)' when it stays. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
OBJECTS = [
    ("logs/app.log", 45),
    ("backups/db.bak", 400),
    ("assets/logo.png", 10),
    ("exports/q1.csv", 120),
]

def target_class(age_days):
    # TODO: return 'ARCHIVE' for age >= 365, 'COLDLINE' for age >= 90,
    #       'NEARLINE' for age >= 30, else 'STANDARD'
    pass

for name, age in OBJECTS:
    # TODO: print '<name> (<age>d): STANDARD -> <target>'
    #       or '<name> (<age>d): STANDARD (no change)' when target is STANDARD
    pass
practice_challenges.expected_output:
logs/app.log (45d): STANDARD -> NEARLINE
backups/db.bak (400d): STANDARD -> ARCHIVE
assets/logo.png (10d): STANDARD (no change)
exports/q1.csv (120d): STANDARD -> COLDLINE

en: Check thresholds from coldest to warmest: 365 first, then 90, then 30 — first match wins.
Return the string 'STANDARD' when no threshold matches, and branch your print on that value.
f"{name} ({age}d): STANDARD -> {cls}" — note the 'd' suffix directly after the age.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert target_class(400) == "ARCHIVE"
assert target_class(365) == "ARCHIVE"
assert target_class(120) == "COLDLINE"
assert target_class(90) == "COLDLINE"
assert target_class(45) == "NEARLINE"
assert target_class(30) == "NEARLINE"
assert target_class(29) == "STANDARD"
assert target_class(0) == "STANDARD"

q.en: What differs between Standard and Archive storage classes?

en: Archive has hours-long retrieval latency like tape
The pricing model: Archive is cheaper at rest but has retrieval fees and a 365-day minimum duration

q.en: A lifecycle rule can do which of the following?

en: Promote Coldline objects back to Standard
Move objects to a colder class or delete them based on conditions like age
Encrypt objects with a new key
Rename objects to match a prefix

q.en: You must guarantee audit logs cannot be deleted or overwritten for 7 years, even by admins. Which feature?
Why is uniform bucket-level access the recommended mode?

en: Access is governed by IAM alone — no per-object ACLs to audit or drift
